Biochemistry: Concepts and Connections

Chapter 17
Interorgan and Intracellular Coordination of Energy Metabolism in Vertebrates
Dean R. Appling
Spencer J. Anthony-Cahill
Christopher K. Mathews

Outline
17.1	Interdependence of the Major Organs in Vertebrate Fuel Metabolism
17.2	Hormonal Regulation of Fuel Metabolism
17.3	Responses to Metabolic Stress: Starvation, Diabetes
17.1 Interdependence of the Major Organs in Vertebrate Fuel Metabolism

Major Fuel-Metabolizing Organs
Brain, muscle, liver, adipose tissue, and heart are the major fuel-metabolizing organs
Triacylglycerols (adipose tissue), protein (skeletal muscle), and glycogen (liver and muscle) are the major fuel reserves
Table 17.1 Profiles of the major vertebrate organs in fuel metabolism

Metabolic Interactions Among the Major Fuel-Metabolizing Organs
Figure 17.1 Metabolic interactions among the major fuel-metabolizing organs.

Fuel Characteristics of Major Organs (1 of 6)
Brain:
fastidious, requires 120 g/day of glucose
60% of a resting human’s daily consumption of glucose
20% of total oxygen consumption
no fuel reserves, but brain can adapt to fasting and use ketone bodies

Fuel Characteristics of Major Organs (2 of 6)
Muscle:
uses glucose, fatty acids, ketone bodies
resting—fatty acids are primary fuel, exertion—stored glycogen is primary fuel (muscle also possesses creatine phosphate stores to regenerate A T P)
lacks glucose 6-phosphatase, so glucose released by glycogenolysis cannot be exported for use by other tissues

Fuel Characteristics of Major Organs (3 of 6)
Muscle:
lactate is released during exertion because glycolysis produces pyruvate faster than the citric acid cycle can use it
Heart:
work output less variable than that of skeletal muscle - and continuous
highly aerobic, no energy reserves except low [creatine phosphate]
requires continuous supply of fuel and oxygen

Fuel Characteristics of Major Organs (4 of 6)
Adipose tissue:
major fuel depot through stored T A G s (enough calories to supply the whole body for two months or more)
some glucose metabolism must occur for synthesis of glycerol-3-phosphate for T A G synthesis; when [glucose] falls, free fatty acids are released for use by other tissues

Fuel Characteristics of Major Organs (5 of 6)
Liver:
synthesis of fuel components for other tissues: fatty acid synthesis and glucose synthesis, both from glycogen mobilization and gluconeogenesis
malonyl-C o A levels regulate fatty acid metabolism through inhibition of carnitine acyltransferase I, controlling fatty acid transport into mitochondria

Fuel Characteristics of Major Organs (6 of 6)
Liver:
buffers blood glucose partly through action of a high
hexokinase (“glucokinase”) and partly through control of the intracellular location of glucose transporters
Blood (connects organs):
vehicle of gas transport and metabolite transport
erythrocytes represent about half the total blood volume meet their energy needs primarily from glycolysis; no mitochondria in matured cells
17.2 Hormonal Regulation of Fuel Metabolism

Control of Fuel Metabolism in Mammals (1 of 3)
Maintenance of blood glucose levels is particularly critical to brain function
Major hormones:
Insulin
Glucagon
Epinephrine
Figure 17.2 Aspects of the control of blood glucose levels by pancreatic secretion of insulin and glucagon.

Control of Fuel Metabolism in Mammals (2 of 3)
Table 17.2 Major hormones controlling fuel metabolism in mammals

Control of Fuel Metabolism in Mammals (3 of 3)
Table 17.2 Major hormones controlling fuel metabolism in mammals

Actions of Glucagon That Lead to an Increase in Blood Glucose Levels
Figure 17.3 Actions of glucagon in liver that lead to a rise in blood glucose.

Increase of Blood Glucose Levels by Epinephrine
Epinephrine is released .by adrenal medulla in response to low blood glucose levels
It reacts with second messenger systems:
Muscle – activates adenylate cyclase and glycogenolysis
Adipose tissue – simulates breakdown of T A G
Pancreas – inhibits insulin secretion, stimulates glucagon secretion
Unlike glucagon, epinephrine effects are short-lived
It also functions as a neurotransmitter

Coordination of Energy Homeostasis (1 of 7)
A M P-activated protein kinase (A M P K)
a serine/threonine kinase that is activated when energy charge is low (high A M P levels)
stimulates pathways that lead to A T P production, while inhibiting A T P-utilizing pathways

Coordination of Energy Homeostasis (2 of 7)
Structure of the mammalian A M P K
Figure 17.4 Mammalian A M P-activated protein kinase (A M P K).

Coordination of Energy Homeostasis (3 of 7)
Mammalian target of rapamycin (m T O R)
like A M P K, a protein serine/threonine kinase found in all eukaryotes
active only under nutrient-rich conditions
promotes anabolic processes – cell proliferation, protein synthesis, lipid biosynthesis
was discovered during studies on the action of rapamycin, an immunosuppressant

Coordination of Energy Homeostasis (4 of 7)
AMPK and mTOR signaling pathways
Figure 17.5 A M P K and m T O R signaling pathways.

Coordination of Energy Homeostasis (5 of 7)
Sirtuins:
are a family of
-dependent protein deacetylases,
removing acetyl groups from protein lysine residues
are activated by high ratios of
and serve
as cellular redox state sensors
metabolic reprogramming proteins such as PGC-1α can be deacetylated by sirtuins, upregulating gluconeogenesis and mitochondrial biogenesis processes

Coordination of Energy Homeostasis (6 of 7)
Sirtuin reaction

Coordination of Energy Homeostasis (7 of 7)
SIRT1 (a sirtuin) and PGC-1α control the reprogramming of fuel utilization in response to fasting
Figure 17.6 P G C-1α and S I R T1 control the reprogramming of fuel utilization pathways in response to fasting.

Energy Generation Strategies in Proliferating (Abundant Nutrients) versus Nonproliferating (Scarce Nutrients) Cells (1 of 3)
AMPK, mTOR, and sirtuins evolved in unicellular organisms
Figure 17.7 Proliferating and nonproliferating cells use different metabolic strategies to generate energy.

Energy Generation Strategies in Proliferating (Abundant Nutrients) versus Nonproliferating (Scarce Nutrients) Cells (2 of 3)
Nondividing (nonproliferating) mammalian cells typically use oxidative metabolism to generate energy
Stimulation by growth factors causes differentiated cells to switch to glycolysis
Warburg (1925) noted rapidly dividing cancer cells metabolize glucose by aerobic glycolysis, but produce lactate rather than pyruvate (Warburg effect)

Energy Generation Strategies in Proliferating (Abundant Nutrients) versus Nonproliferating (Scarce Nutrients) Cells (3 of 3)
Figure 17.7 Proliferating and nonproliferating cells use different metabolic strategies to generate energy.

Fuel and Hormonal Control of Appetite (1 of 2)
In mammals, the brain coordinates whole-body energy homeostasis
A M P K activation promotes food intake
Insulin acts through PI3K (phosphoinositide 3-kinase) to inhibit food intake
Figure 17.8 Fuel and hormonal control of appetite in the arcuate nucleus of the hypothalamus.
F F A = free fatty acids; B C A A = branched chain amino acids

Fuel and Hormonal Control of Appetite (2 of 2)
Leptin has direct and indirect effects (via PI3K and  A M P K)
Adiponectin and ghrelin stimulate food intake via A M P K
Figure 17.8 Fuel and hormonal control of appetite in the arcuate nucleus of the hypothalamus.
F F A = free fatty acids; B C A A = branched chain amino acids
17.3 Responses to Metabolic Stress: Starvation, Diabetes

Major Events in the Storage and Use of Fuels in the Fed State, Unfed State, and Early Starvation
Figure 17.9 Major events in the storage, retrieval, and use of fuels in the fed and unfed states and in early starvation.

Diabetes Mellitus (1 of 2)
is a family of diseases resulting from insulin deficiency or defects in insulin response
The biochemical response to starvation is similar to the response to diabetes mellitus, in that alternative fuel sources must be found, because there is essentially a glucose deficiency

Diabetes Mellitus (2 of 2)
There are two common types of diabetes mellitus
Type 1 diabetes is caused by insulin deficiency (e.g., autoimmune destruction of pancreas β cells, mutation in insulin receptor, etc.)
Type 1 diabetes can be treated with (injections of) insulin

Metabolic Abnormalities in Diabetes
Figure 17.10 The metabolic abnormalities in diabetes.

Chapter 17 Summary (1 of 2)
Each organ or tissue of a multicellular organism has a distinctive profile of metabolic activities that allows it to serve its specialized functions
However, these tissues and organs must remain in constant communication to maintain homeostasis with respect to levels of nutrients (glucose), energy (A T P) and other factors
Glucose homeostasis is mainly controlled by the actions of three hormones – insulin, glucagon, and epinephrine

Chapter 17 Summary (2 of 2)
Two protein kinases, A M P K and m T O R, play central roles in orchestrating the metabolic activities of mammalian cells important for energy homeostasis by responding to A M P/A T P ratios, nutrient-poor/rich conditions, etc
Sirtuins act as metabolic sensors of the cellular redox state
